security_hardening.title: Add constant-time token comparison to prevent timing attacks
security_hardening.description: The BOP_SERVE_TOKEN comparison in serve.rs uses direct string equality (auth != bearer), which is vulnerable to timing attacks. An attacker could use response timing to guess the token character by character.
security_hardening.rationale: Timing attacks allow attackers to extract secrets by measuring how long comparisons take. Constant-time comparison ensures all comparisons take the same time regardless of where strings differ, preventing this attack vector.
security_hardening.currentRisk: Attacker can potentially extract BOP_SERVE_TOKEN through timing analysis of authentication requests
security_hardening.remediation: Use constant_time_eq from the subtle crate or implement a constant-time comparison. Replace line 45 with: use subtle::ConstantTimeEq; if auth.as_bytes().ct_eq(bearer.as_bytes()).into() { ... }

security_hardening.title: Fix command injection vulnerability in acceptance criteria execution
security_hardening.description: merge_gate.rs executes acceptance criteria using sh -lc with user-provided strings (line 118-120). This allows arbitrary command execution if an attacker can control the acceptance_criteria field in meta.json.
security_hardening.rationale: Command injection is a critical vulnerability allowing full system compromise. The acceptance_criteria array is user-controlled and executed directly via shell without sanitization or escaping.
security_hardening.currentRisk: Malicious cards with crafted acceptance_criteria can execute arbitrary commands during merge-gate processing
security_hardening.remediation: 1) Restrict acceptance_criteria to predefined safe commands (allowlist). 2) Parse and validate commands before execution. 3) Use a restricted shell or container for execution. 4) Add schema validation requiring approval for shell commands in criteria.

security_hardening.title: Add rate limiting to bop serve HTTP endpoint
security_hardening.description: The bop serve HTTP server at /cards/new has no rate limiting. An attacker can flood the endpoint with card creation requests, causing resource exhaustion or filling disk space with malicious cards.
security_hardening.rationale: Without rate limiting, the service is vulnerable to denial of service attacks and resource exhaustion. Rate limiting is a fundamental security control for public-facing APIs.
security_hardening.vulnerability: CWE-770: Allocation of Resources Without Limits
security_hardening.currentRisk: Attacker can create unlimited cards via POST /cards/new, exhausting disk space and system resources
security_hardening.remediation: Implement rate limiting using tower-governor or similar middleware. Add configurable limits: e.g., 10 requests/minute per IP, 100 requests/hour globally. Example: use tower_governor::{GovernorLayer, GovernorConfig};

security_hardening.title: Make BOP_SERVE_TOKEN authentication mandatory by default
security_hardening.description: serve.rs makes token authentication optional via BOP_SERVE_TOKEN env var. If not set, the server runs with no authentication, allowing anyone to create cards. The default should be secure (auth required).
security_hardening.rationale: Security defaults should be 'deny by default'. Running an unauthenticated HTTP service that accepts card creation is a significant security risk. Administrators may forget to set BOP_SERVE_TOKEN in production.
security_hardening.currentRisk: Production servers may run without authentication if BOP_SERVE_TOKEN is not explicitly set, allowing unauthorized card creation
security_hardening.remediation: 1) Fail to start if BOP_SERVE_TOKEN is not set (or require --allow-unauthenticated flag). 2) Generate a random token on first run if none provided. 3) Add startup warning if running without auth.

security_hardening.title: Add request body size limits to prevent DoS
security_hardening.description: The bop serve endpoint accepts JSON requests with no maximum size limit. An attacker can send extremely large spec or id fields, causing memory exhaustion or application crashes.
security_hardening.rationale: Unbounded input sizes are a common DoS vector. Large JSON payloads can exhaust memory, cause parser slowdowns, or crash the service. Industry best practice is to enforce strict size limits.
security_hardening.currentRisk: Attacker can send multi-GB JSON requests causing OOM or service degradation
security_hardening.remediation: Add axum DefaultBodyLimit layer with reasonable limit (e.g., 1MB for card specs). Example: .layer(DefaultBodyLimit::max(1024 * 1024))

security_hardening.title: Implement secrets detection in policy check
security_hardening.description: The policy check in policy.rs verifies file counts and LOC but does not scan for hardcoded secrets (API keys, tokens, passwords). Secrets can be accidentally committed and merged.
security_hardening.rationale: Hardcoded secrets in version control are a critical security risk. Once committed, secrets remain in git history even if later removed. Automated detection prevents accidental exposure.
security_hardening.currentRisk: Developers can commit API keys, tokens, and passwords to the repository without detection, exposing credentials in version control
security_hardening.remediation: Add regex-based secret scanning to run_staged_policy_check(). Check for patterns: AWS keys (AKIA...), tokens (sk-...), private keys (BEGIN.*PRIVATE KEY), passwords in config. Return policy violation if detected.

security_hardening.title: Add cargo audit to CI/CD pipeline for dependency scanning
security_hardening.description: The project has no automated dependency vulnerability scanning. cargo audit is not installed or run in the build pipeline, allowing vulnerable dependencies to be used in production.
security_hardening.rationale: Vulnerable dependencies are a top attack vector (OWASP A06). cargo audit checks Cargo.lock against the RustSec advisory database to identify known CVEs. This should be a required CI check.
security_hardening.vulnerability: CWE-1104: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components
security_hardening.currentRisk: Project may be using dependencies with known critical vulnerabilities (CVEs) without detection
security_hardening.remediation: 1) Add cargo install cargo-audit to CI setup. 2) Run cargo audit in make check target. 3) Add GitHub Action: RustSec/audit-check@v1. 4) Fail builds on high/critical vulnerabilities.

security_hardening.title: Add security headers to HTTP responses
security_hardening.description: serve.rs returns HTTP responses without security headers like X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy, or Strict-Transport-Security. These headers prevent common attacks.
security_hardening.rationale: Security headers are defense-in-depth protections against XSS, clickjacking, MIME-sniffing, and protocol downgrade attacks. They're required by security standards and best practices.
security_hardening.currentRisk: Missing security headers increase attack surface for browser-based attacks if the API is accessed via browser
security_hardening.remediation: Add tower-http SecurityHeadersLayer middleware. Set headers: X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, X-Frame-Options: DENY, Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'none', X-XSS-Protection: 1; mode=block

security_hardening.title: Sanitize and validate card ID to prevent path traversal
security_hardening.description: serve.rs validates card ID to reject '/' and '\' (lines 61-66), but this validation is incomplete. Unicode normalization, encoded slashes (%2F), and other path traversal techniques are not blocked.
security_hardening.rationale: Path traversal vulnerabilities allow attackers to write files outside intended directories. While basic slash filtering exists, sophisticated attacks using encoding or Unicode can bypass it.
security_hardening.currentRisk: Potential for sophisticated path traversal attacks using encoded characters or Unicode normalization
security_hardening.remediation: Strengthen ID validation: 1) Allow only alphanumeric + hyphens/underscores. 2) Reject encoded characters (%XX). 3) Apply Unicode normalization (NFC) before validation. 4) Add max length check (e.g., 200 chars).

security_hardening.title: Implement audit logging for security-relevant events
security_hardening.description: The system has no audit logging for security events like authentication failures, card creation, policy violations, or merge-gate rejections. Security incidents cannot be investigated retroactively.
security_hardening.rationale: Audit logs are essential for security monitoring, incident response, and compliance. Without logs, detecting breaches, investigating incidents, or proving compliance is impossible.
security_hardening.currentRisk: Security incidents (unauthorized access, policy violations, failed authentication) go undetected and cannot be investigated
security_hardening.remediation: 1) Create structured audit log (.cards/audit.jsonl). 2) Log: auth failures, card creation (who/when/what), policy violations, acceptance criteria failures. 3) Include timestamp, source IP, user, action, result. 4) Add bop audit command to query logs.

security_hardening.title: Restrict provider environment variable exposure to adapters
security_hardening.description: dispatcher.rs passes provider_env variables directly to adapter child processes (line 612). If provider credentials are in ENV, they're exposed to untrusted adapter scripts and inherited by all subprocess commands.
security_hardening.rationale: Environment variables are inherited by all child processes and can leak through error messages, core dumps, or /proc. Sensitive credentials should use more secure mechanisms like secret files with restricted permissions.
security_hardening.currentRisk: Provider API keys and credentials exposed to adapter subprocesses can leak through logs, error dumps, or malicious adapters
security_hardening.remediation: 1) Pass credentials via secure temp files with 0600 permissions instead of ENV. 2) Clear sensitive env vars after use. 3) Document that providers should use file-based credentials. 4) Add provider.json with credential_file field.

security_hardening.title: Add HTTPS/TLS support for bop serve production deployments
security_hardening.description: bop serve only supports plain HTTP on 127.0.0.1. If bound to 0.0.0.0 for remote access, traffic (including BOP_SERVE_TOKEN) is sent in cleartext over the network.
security_hardening.rationale: Unencrypted HTTP exposes authentication tokens and card data to network sniffing attacks. TLS encryption is mandatory for any authentication system to prevent credential theft.
security_hardening.vulnerability: CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
security_hardening.currentRisk: If bop serve is exposed to network (--host 0.0.0.0), authentication tokens and card contents transmitted in cleartext can be intercepted
security_hardening.remediation: Add TLS support: 1) Accept --tls-cert and --tls-key flags. 2) Use axum-server with rustls for TLS termination. 3) Require TLS if host != 127.0.0.1. 4) Document reverse proxy setup (nginx/Caddy) for production.

security_hardening.title: Implement adapter script signature verification
security_hardening.description: Adapters in adapters/*.nu are executed with full privileges but have no integrity checks. A compromised or malicious adapter can execute arbitrary code with dispatcher permissions.
security_hardening.rationale: Adapters are a critical trust boundary - they execute untrusted prompts and have access to the full filesystem and environment. Code signing ensures only approved adapters can run.
security_hardening.vulnerability: CWE-494: Download of Code Without Integrity Check
security_hardening.currentRisk: Malicious actors can replace adapter scripts to execute arbitrary code when dispatcher runs cards
security_hardening.remediation: 1) Add adapter checksums to config (SHA256 hashes). 2) Verify hash before execution. 3) Require --allow-unsigned-adapters flag to bypass. 4) Add bop adapter sign command to update hashes. 5) Consider code signing with GPG.

security_hardening.title: Add input validation and sanitization for template parameter
security_hardening.description: cards.rs create_card() accepts a template parameter that's used to construct file paths (e.g., .cards/templates/{template}.bop). While path separators are checked, there's no comprehensive validation of template names.
security_hardening.rationale: Template names are used in path construction and could potentially be exploited for path traversal or to load unintended templates. Strict validation prevents abuse.
security_hardening.vulnerability: CWE-73: External Control of File Name or Path
security_hardening.currentRisk: Insufficient validation of template parameter could allow path traversal or unintended template access
security_hardening.remediation: Add strict template name validation: 1) Allow only alphanumeric + hyphen/underscore. 2) Reject '.', '..', and path separators. 3) Add max length (e.g., 50 chars). 4) Verify template exists in allowed directory before use.

security_hardening.title: Sanitize error messages to prevent information disclosure
security_hardening.description: Error responses in serve.rs and other modules include raw error messages from anyhow (e.g., line 116, 134). These may leak sensitive information like file paths, internal structure, or stack traces.
security_hardening.rationale: Verbose error messages help attackers map system internals, understand file structure, and identify vulnerabilities. Error responses should be generic to users while logging details internally.
security_hardening.vulnerability: CWE-209: Information Exposure Through Error Messages
security_hardening.currentRisk: Error messages may reveal internal paths, configuration, or implementation details useful for reconnaissance
security_hardening.remediation: 1) Return generic error messages to API clients (e.g., 'Card creation failed'). 2) Log detailed errors to audit log. 3) Add --debug flag for verbose errors in development. 4) Never include stack traces in production responses.
